poor_metabolizer.summary: CYP2D6 poor metabolizer status significantly reduces conversion of tamoxifen to its active metabolite endoxifen. This may result in reduced therapeutic efficacy.
discussion_points: Consider alternative endocrine therapy (e.g., aromatase inhibitor if postmenopausal)
CYP2D6 inhibitor co-medications should be reviewed
Endoxifen plasma level monitoring may be informative
Discuss with oncology team regarding dose adjustment or drug switch

intermediate_metabolizer.summary: CYP2D6 intermediate metabolizer status may moderately reduce conversion of tamoxifen to endoxifen. Clinical impact varies.
discussion_points: Monitor clinical response to tamoxifen therapy
Avoid strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g., paroxetine, fluoxetine)
Consider endoxifen level testing if poor response suspected

normal_metabolizer.summary: CYP2D6 normal metabolizer status supports standard tamoxifen dosing. Expected adequate conversion to endoxifen.
discussion_points: Continue to avoid strong CYP2D6 inhibitors

poor_metabolizer.summary: CYP2C19 poor metabolizer status significantly reduces activation of clopidogrel. Patients may not receive adequate antiplatelet effect, increasing cardiovascular risk.
discussion_points: Consider alternative antiplatelet therapy (e.g., prasugrel or ticagrelor)
FDA boxed warning applies for CYP2C19 poor metabolizers
Platelet function testing may inform clinical decisions
poor_metabolizer.evidence: PharmGKB Level 1A - CPIC Guideline / FDA Boxed Warning

intermediate_metabolizer.summary: CYP2C19 intermediate metabolizer status may reduce clopidogrel activation. Effect on clinical outcomes is variable.
discussion_points: Consider alternative therapy if high cardiovascular risk
Platelet function testing may be informative

normal_metabolizer.summary: CYP2C19 normal metabolizer status supports standard clopidogrel dosing with expected antiplatelet effect.
discussion_points: Continue monitoring for bleeding risk as per guidelines

disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and should not replace professional medical advice. Always consult with a healthcare provider or pharmacist for medication decisions.
